data.title: Politics And The Markets 10/22/24
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

Readers interested in investing topics tied to the upcoming election can find more information at the upcoming Seeking Alpha Investing Forum: Election 2024; please visit the event website.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: E2open Parent Holdings: Long-Term Growth Outlook Remains Intact
data.description:
Investment Action

I recommended a buy rating for E2open Parent Holdings (NYSE:ETWO) when I wrote about it in July, as I thought the growth slowdown in 1Q25 was not due to structural reasons. So long as ETWO is able to close the deals that are being pushed out, there shouldn’t be an issue with it continuing to grow. Based on my current outlook and analysis of ETWO, I recommend a buy rating. I still think ETWO presents an attractive investment opportunity, as the weakness in 2Q25 was a temporary one. The underlying demand momentum remains strong, and ETWO should see growth acceleration as it finds more success with its system integrator channel strategy and benefits from the ERP upgrade cycle.

Review

ETWO reported 2Q25 earnings on the 9th of October, where it saw total adj revenue of $152.2 million. Adj subscription revenue contributed $131.6 million, and adj. professional services revenue contributed $20.6 million. Total revenue missed consensus expectation of $154.3 million. In terms of profit, adj gross profit came in at $105 million, while adj EBITDA saw $54.9 million, and adj net income saw $16.6 million. FY25 guidance was lowered across multiple metrics, including total revenue of $612 million at the midpoint vs. $637.5 million prior; adj. EBITDA is now expected to come in at the low end of prior guidance, and professional services (implied from deducting subscription revenue guide from total revenue guide) guidance of $83 million at the midpoint (vs. prior implied guidance of $100.5 million) represents a y/y decline of around 18%. This also marked the lowest professional services revenue since FY21, and also the third consecutive year in which the implied professional services revenue guide has been lowered during the year vs. the original implied guidance.

ETWO performance this quarter was not up to my expectations. The core part of my previous bull thesis was that ETWO needs to execute and convert deals that were pushed out. Unfortunately, that did not happen, and this led to the second consecutive quarter where total revenue has missed expectations. That said, I am still a firm believer that this weakness is a temporary one. According to the call, management noted that the weakness was due to delays in closing large and complex deals amid extended client decision timelines. This essentially led to continued unbilled professional services work, which caused the guide down.

Despite all of this, I think the growth outlook is still strong if we take a longer-term outlook. First, to address the execution issue (in closing sales), I thought that management made the right decision to allocate its sales reps to focus on improving customer retention during the quarter, as I believe retaining a client is more important than closing a new deal, especially in the current uncertain macro environment (and also that prospective clients are delaying decisions). This has played out well, as retention improved both annually and sequentially. While it is true that this made ETWO slower to respond to the deal closing process (and hence the weak growth and guide), the fact that ETWO managed to deliver improved bookings tells us how strong underlying demand actually is. The strength behind the booking growth was also notable as ETWO closed two key deals, with the largest deal in the quarter from a leading global manufacturer of high technology components (ETWO won this through cross-selling, so it clearly demonstrates the demand potential from existing clients), and the other large new logo deal with a global membership-based retailer (ETWO is still able to win new clients despite allocating lesser sales reps to close new deals).

Looking ahead, there are also two drivers in place that should accelerate ETWO growth. Firstly, ETWO is seeing strong traction in its system integrator channel, where they have gone from no partnerships to a couple of partnerships now over 15 major client accounts. This is important to note because it means ETWO is able to target a wider group of clients and also potentially leverage on these integrators to close deals that ETWO was not able to (due to the allocation of sales reps to retain existing clients).

Secondly, I think we are in the early innings of a major ERP upgrade cycle, and ETWO is set to benefit from this. According to Morgan Stanley’s research (using Alphasense), the number of times “ERP Update” was mentioned in company transcripts has spiked to an all-time high, and based on their survey, 50% of CIOs expect to upgrade their ERP within the next 24 months. We can already see this benefiting ETWO, as two of the wins in the quarter were initiated from an SAP-related evaluation. Given ETWO's unique SAP capabilities, such as its proprietary API, I expect ETWO to be well positioned to capture more of such migration opportunities. I think management comment that they are seeing momentum show up in the pipeline is a very encouraging sign as well.

Valuation

I still think the bull case is intact for ETWO. Given the near-term uncertainties, I think it makes more sense to have a longer-term view. In this model, I focused on ETWO FY28 performance, where I expect growth to recover back to low teens (above 1Q23’s level where the business performance started to deteriorate). My FY25 estimates follow the midpoint of management's revised FY25 guide, and FY26/27 halved from my previous assumption to reflect a more conservative view. I note to readers that ETWO was a high-growth business pre-covid and the high rate environment (look at pre-FY23 performance). While growth may not recover to previous height, I think it is very possible for growth to recover back to low teens given the improving macro condition (rates came down), the new system integrator channel that is working, and the ERP upgrade cycle. If growth can recover back to low teens, I think it should minimally trade at where it traded in 1Q23 (1CQ22) ~3x forward P/S ratio. At this multiple, I see a substantial upside to the stock price today.

Risk

The market may not attach any value to ETWO’s long-term performance if management continues to disappoint vs. consensus expectations. This is a risk that investors need to bear today as it is hard to accurately forecast the near-term performance, and investors are less likely to be forgiving given two quarters of misses so far. Aside from expectations, ETWO may not benefit as much as I believe it should from the current ERP upgrade cycle, and this could limit ETWO’s ability to accelerate growth.

Final Thoughts

My recommendation is a buy for ETWO. While the business continues to face challenges in closing large deals and delivering on near-term expectations, I believe the underlying fundamentals remain strong. The traction with its new system integrator partnerships strategy and the ERP upgrade cycle should help ETWO accelerate growth. As such, while the market may be cautious in the short term, I think investors with a longer-term horizon can take advantage of the cheap valuation today.

data.title: ASML: Investors Have Been Served A Proof Point Of Feeble Demand
data.description:
I took a risk in late 2023 arguing that it is "not the time to be bullish" on ASML (NASDAQ:ASML) stock, defying a strong and evident boom in selected AI-related verticals within the semiconductor industry. I wrote:

Today [...] I continue to remain more bearish than bullish [on ASML]. Specifically, looking at the overall semiconductor landscape, especially the equipment side, I warn against turning too bullish, too soon. As I see it, the equipment CAPEX investment cycle for most foundry companies has peaked sometime during the past 12–18 months.

Since the argument was published, ASML stock has returned a feeble 4%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) has surged more than 27%.

In my view, ASML's recent Q3 results delivered a clear proof point that semiconductor equipment demand is still not seeing the light at the end of the tunnel. Several factors, specifically Intel's CAPEX cuts, Samsung's foundry troubles, and China's weakening demand, are putting pressure on ASML's growth trajectory. Despite holding a near-monopoly in EUV lithography tools, ASML faces the challenge of overcapacity and risks tied to customer CAPEX spending reductions. I remain "Hold" rated.

Q3 Results Do Not Give Me Confidence ...

ASML reported Q3 results for 2024 on October 15th and shares dropped sharply (as much as 15%) on the announcement. Indeed, Q3 results raised some notable red flags for investors regarding the current state of semiconductor equipment demand. During the period spanning from June through end of September, ASML generated about €7.5 billion in revenues, up about 21% YoY compared to the same period in 2023. The increase in sales was driven by a jump in new lithography systems sold (+17 units YoY). Gross profit grew 19% YoY, to €3.8 billion, and net income grew 31% YoY, to €2.1 billion. Although ASML's results highlight notable growth YoY, I highlight that the comp in 2023 was very weak, and results - especially guidance - were below expectations on weaker-than-expected recovery trends.

... With Weak Guidance Adding To The Negativity

While ASML's Q3 results were okayish. The big negative surprise was in the guidance: The company revised its 2025 sales guidance range to €29.5 billion to €32.5 billion, reflecting a notable reduction from the previous range of €30 billion to €35 billion​. This revision primarily resulted from significant cuts in EUV and DUV orders on the backdrop of ongoing semiconductor down-cycle, and delays in fab ramp-ups, as well as geopolitical tensions. For context, ASML’s initial guidance for EUV tool shipments was in the range of 60 to 85 units. However, the company revised this projection down by approximately 20 unit, to an estimated 50 units for 2025. At the same time, ASML now expects DUV revenues to decline by 19% YoY in 2025​. This decline is largely driven by the normalization of demand from China, which accounts for a significant portion of DUV sales. In 2024, approximately 48% of DUV revenue came from China, and this is expected to drop to around 20% by 2025​. Paired with the lower revenue outlook, negative operating leverage should bite profitability sharply in 2025: The gross margin forecast was revised down by approximately 2.4 percentage points to 52.2% for 2025​. In my view, it will definitely take some time until shareholder confidence recovers against such a big guidance disappointment, but a first confidence anchor could be given in context of the upcoming Capital Markets Day.

Looking Ahead: The CMD and 2030 Outlook

ASML’s upcoming Capital Markets Day on November 14th will be crucial in shaping investor sentiment for 2026 and beyond. The CMD will focus on ASML's longer-term strategies and its 2030 model, which assumes a more normalized market environment​. There are 2 key discussion points that I am looking for in the CMD presentation: Firstly, I am interested to hear how management expects to bank on new technology cycles like AI-related demand - because so far, the tailwind has not played out for the company. At the same time, I am interested to understand how ASML sees the potential for demand acceleration in North America, the core AI market. This is also important considering that China is slowly breaking off as a customer region. Secondly, I hope to learn more how ASML expects to drive recovery in DUV outside China. This is especially relevant as the company has acknowledged that rising innovations could reduce lithography usage, such as cuLitho and ILT, which could impact demand over time.

Stock Still Trading At A Premium

Although ASML is navigating a challenging commercial backdrop, I highlight that shares are still trading at a premium compared to information technology sector. Indeed, ASML stock is currently trading at 30x EV/EBIT for 2025 estimates. This, in my view, is expensive - even for companies with monopoly-like market power: Investors should consider that the EV/EBIT range for the Mag 7 stocks trends closer to 25x to >30x (GOOG 17x, Meta 23x, MSFT 25x, AAPL 28x, AMZN 33x, NVDA 40x, TSLA 93x). As I have written in my previous coverage on ASML, I continue to be looking for earnings multiples between 20-25x as a potential entry point.

Investor Takeaway

ASML’s Q3 2024 results provide clear proof that semiconductor equipment demand is not seeing the light at the end of the tunnel. With reduced EUV shipments, declining China revenues, and uncertainty in the foundry and memory segments, ASML faces an uphill battle in the near term. On that note, the upcoming CMD will be critical in outlining ASML’s long-term vision and addressing investor concerns. For now, however, I remain "Hold" rated.

data.title: Mitsui & Co.: More Risk Than Reward, Beware The Winds Of Change
data.description:
Japan’s Mitsui & Co. (OTCPK:MITSY) (OTCPK:MITSF), well-known for being one of Berkshire Hathaway’s (BRK.B) trading company picks, has evolved from the more resources-heavy of the group to a far more diversified conglomerate today. Still, what Mitsui hasn’t diversified away is its cyclicality – the result of an earnings base primarily linked to Mineral & Metal Resources (iron ore), Energy (LNG and crude oil), as well as Machinery & Infrastructure (autos and trucks). In essence, Mitsui’s fortunes are tied to global manufacturing, which, per global purchasing manager indicators, remains quite sluggish.

To be clear, Mitsui should still generate a good amount of earnings, certainly enough to fund its capital allocation plans. Meanwhile, like the rest of Japan, there’s an improving balance sheet management angle, thanks to the Tokyo Stock Exchange’s governance reforms.

That said, more positives than negatives are, in my view, priced into the shares, which, having outperformed broader Japanese indices to date, trade at a historically wide premium to book. And with all signs pointing to 'hawkish' new PM Ishiba winning a big majority at the general election this month, I’d err on the side of caution here.

Japan is Poised for a 'Hawkish' Regime Change

At the macro level, the key new development in Japan is that Shigeru Ishiba has, against expectations, won the Liberal Democratic Party (or ‘LDP’) leadership race. Ishiba’s victory is far from a symbolic one, as it marks a passing of the torch from the ‘Abenomics’ faction (i.e., big fiscal spending, ultra-loose monetary policy), led by runner-up Sanae Takaichi, to a more economically prudent philosophy.

A more ‘hawkish’ economic regime in Japan is, with the exception of banks (see Sumitomo Mitsui: Many Ways To Win), a net negative for Japanese corporates. This negative readthrough applies to global conglomerates like Mitsui as well.

Yes, fiscal cuts are not as much of a concern, given Mitsui, like the other trading companies, derives a significant portion of its profits from outside Japan. Still, the company won’t be exempt from corporate tax hikes, something that Ishiba is in favor of; nor will it be immune from a stronger yen should we see a structural shift toward tighter fiscal and monetary policies. Also worth keeping in mind is that Ishiba has been in favor of tax hikes on capital gains or financial products in the past, either of which could interrupt Mitsui’s progress on unwinding its cross-shareholdings.

In any case, this month’s general election is a key monitorable. Current polling indicates an Ishiba-led LDP is on track to retain, if not expand, its majority, which would be good for stability. But this would also allow him to push through the more politically challenging (and investor unfriendly) fiscal policies on his agenda. Hence, a potentially negative outcome for Mitsui.

Mitsui’s Earnings Momentum May Be Slowing

The Japanese macro might be turning against Mitsui, but the bigger concern for now is that the company’s earnings power may already be eroding. Recall from FQ1 2025 that strong headline net profits (31% vs fiscal year guidance of JPY900bn) were boosted by quite a few one-offs.

Top of mind is Mitsui’s disposal of its stake in Indonesian coal-fired power producer Paiton Energy, which boosted Machinery & Infrastructure segment net profits. Other benefits included favorable commodity prices and FX, particularly important for the Mineral & Metal Resources, as well as asset recycling gains. Adjusting for these one-offs, “base” net profits were, despite strong iron ore volumes, actually down YoY, as was operating cash generation.

From here, the setup into fiscal Q2 results (scheduled for 1st November) isn’t ideal. For one, the two key commodity price cycles Mitsui is exposed to, iron ore and crude oil prices, are, despite help from Chinese stimulus optimism, still on the decline.

External conditions also aren’t exactly moving in Mitsui’s favor. Firstly, ongoing slowdowns in major global economies (per forward-looking PMI data). Secondly, from a strengthening yen post-LDP election and amid diverging monetary policies between Japan (tightening) and the US (loosening).

Nor is the regulatory backdrop, as evidenced by ongoing troubles at Arctic LNG-2, a project which got hit by expanded US Treasury sanctions last month and where Mitsui last disclosed a JPY372.7bn exposure (net of provisions).

Of course, the group is diversified enough, by region and across the value chain, that it should still generate earnings through the cycles. That said, the rate of change may well be turning for the worse, and with the stock still up quite a bit this year, I would be cautious about expectations heading into Mitsui’s upcoming results.

Maxed Out on Capital Returns

With Japanese stocks selling off across the board in July/August amid unwinding carry trades, management opportunistically announced two new capital return policy updates. Firstly, additional share buybacks on top of its existing program, which raise the maximum repurchase amount to JPY400bn (vs. JPY200bn previously) or ~6% of outstanding shares. And secondly, extending its buyback period to February 2025 (vs September 2024 previously).

Now, Mitsui retains enough balance sheet capacity that more buybacks won’t hurt its creditworthiness. The size of this new program does, however, limit the space for incremental capital returns going forward. For context, the outlook for this fiscal year is now for JPY400bn in buybacks, on top of a dividend payment of JPY295bn. Combined, this comes up to a total shareholder return ratio of 77% (as a % of net profit guidance) – nearly double the >40% target laid out in management’s mid-term plan.

To be fair, Mitsui hasn’t fully optimized its balance sheet, so there are still a few levers management can pull. For instance, by unwinding its web of equity cross-shareholdings at a faster pace. Given the relatively gradual pace at which the company has progressed on this front, though, I wouldn’t pencil in any more upside catalysts to an already elevated high-single-digit % total shareholder return yield (buybacks + dividend).

Summing Up

The winds of change are gaining pace domestically and externally. For the most part, though, not in Mitsui’s favor. While earnings are diversified enough to tide through cyclical turns and support the yield, there likely isn’t much room for more share price catalysts from here. Having already re-rated to a ~20% book value premium, the Mitsui risk/reward isn’t particularly attractive here.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Trinity Capital: Is This 15% Yield Safe?
data.description:
Business development companies are popular with dividend investors for obvious reasons: they pay high dividends, and in many cases yields are in double-digit territory. Trinity Capital (NASDAQ:TRIN) stands out of the BDC universe by offering dividend investors a massive 15% yield, which begs the question as to how safe this yield is. Since a couple of other BDCs have recently highly disappointed -- like TriplePoint Venture Growth (TPVG) and Oaktree Specialty Lending (OCSL) -- I believe investors should be careful to invest in companies purely for their high yields. I will delve into Trinity Capital and clarify whether or not the BDC has the potential to sustain its dividend, or whether dividend investors must expect a dividend cut.

Trinity Capital's business proposition, net investment income trend and coverage analysis

Trinity Capital is an internally managed BDC and is focused on providing debt and equipment financing to growth stage companies. As of the end of the June quarter, Trinity Capital's portfolio was valued at $1.43B (based off of fair value) and the BDC had total assets of $1.51B. The primary source of income for Trinity Capital are variable rate, secured loans to companies, mainly given to companies in the technology sector. Loans represented 70.4% of investments and generated 72.3% of investment income for Trinity Capital in the second-quarter. Equipment financing, a separate income stream, was responsible for 23.3% of investments and 17.6% of portfolio income.

According to Trinity Capital’s Q3’24 pre-release for the third quarter, the BDC funded $459 million in new investments in Q3. This total included $406M in secured loans, $39M in equipment financings and $14M in warrants and other equity investments. Total originations amounted to $635M, a new record for Trinity Capital.

In terms of yields, Trinity Capital's effective investments yields have risen to 16% in the third fiscal quarter, showing 0.2 PP growth quarter-over-quarter. The BDC is obviously benefiting from a strong lending environment where demand for loans is high and yields prove to be stable. I believe this works to the benefit of Trinity Capital and the broader BDC sector as well.

Trinity Capital's loans and equipment financing deals generated $26.7M in net investment income in the June quarter, showing 21% year-over-year growth. The investment firm is clearly growing, in part due to high demand for new originations in the technology sector. The BDC, however, on a per-share basis, has seen declining net investment income, which poses a potential challenge for the dividend going forward.

Trinity Capital’s dividend coverage profile is not bad, but it is not great either. In Q2’24, the BDC generated net investment income of $0.53 per-share which calculates, based off of a dividend payout of $0.51 per-share, to a coverage ratio of 1.04X. In the previous quarter, the dividend coverage ratio was 1.06X and in the trailing twelve months period, Trinity Capital’s dividend coverage ratio calculates to 1.10X. Trinity Capital’s net investment income steadily declined in the last four quarters, and the dividend coverage ratio also consistently declined.

Trinity Capital returns income exceeding its regular distribution requirements to shareholders through special dividend payments, but the BDC did not pay any special dividends in the last three quarters, potentially because of lower safety margin reflected in its dividend coverage ratio. For now, I believe the dividend is reasonably well protected, but other examples in the tech-focused BDC market have shown that high yields should be viewed with suspicion: Get Ready For A Dividend Cut.

Trinity Capital’s valuation

Trinity Capital is valued at a price-to-book ratio of 1.09X, which makes the BDC likely about fairly valued given that its P/B ratio is at about the same level as the 3-year price-to-book ratio. The BDC has traded at a higher price-to-BV ratio last year, which was when Trinity Capital returned more cash to shareholders through its special dividends and BDCs did overall well. However, tech-focused BDCs have seen a growing valuation pressure, especially because some BDCs suffered investment losses and a deterioration in their balance sheet quality. The industry group average P/B ratio is 1.19X (including HTGC) and 0.99X (excluding HTGC) book value. Given the declining NII trend and weakening coverage, I would be comfortable with a 1.0X P/B ratio, implying a fair value of ~12.80/share.

As a result, TriplePoint Venture Growth was forced to cut its dividend by 25% in light of lower net investment income. Hercules Capital (HTGC), which is the leading BDC in the venture capital market, has avoided these troubles and continues to trade by far at the highest (77%) premium to book value. Hercules Capital is a quality BDC given its consistent dividend coverage and portfolio growth... and I currently rate it as a buy.

Risks with Trinity Capital

Trinity Capital carries a mix of fixed and variable rate loans on its balance sheet. Variable rate loans represented just 70% of investments as of the end of the June quarter, meaning Trinity Capital is not as exposed to falling federal fund rates as other BDCs. Nonetheless, Trinity Capital has considerable exposure to loans that pay variable rates and there is a risk that in a low-rate world, the BDC’s income will fall. Since Trinity Capital’s net investment income has fallen on a per-share basis in the last year and the dividend coverage ratio deteriorated as a result, there is a risk that the BDC may not be able to meet its dividend requirements.

Final thoughts

Trinity Capital’s shares are trading at a 15% yield and the dividend, at least at the current time, is supported by net investment income. The BDC, however, has seen a four-quarter streak of falling net investment income and the dividend coverage ratio has deteriorated as well, leaving only a small safety margin. While the BDC is not yet in dividend trouble, falling portfolio income tied to the investment firm’s variable rate loans could potentially become a problem for the company in FY 2025. Since shares currently trade at the historical P/B ratio, I believe a hold rating makes the most sense here and accounts for the risk that I see for Trinity Capital's investment proposition.

data.title: Analysing Osisko Development's Potential As It Balances Risk And Promise
data.description:
Thesis

Osisko Development Corp. (NYSE:ODV) isn’t exactly a well-known name, but this Montreal mining company has some major projects, like the Cariboo Gold Project in British Columbia, that look promising. That said, things haven’t gone exactly as planned. The company’s stock has dropped hard over the past few years, thanks to financial missteps and operational setbacks.

So while Osisko clearly has some potential, particularly with gold prices inching upward and a couple of hefty projects in the pipeline, they’re also staring down a few formidable challenges: financial strain, shareholder dilution, and operational risks still loom large. In short, it’s not quite time to get too carried away just yet.

Company Overview

Osisko Development Corp. is one of those companies you might not have heard of, but they’re quietly busy breathing life back into old gold mines. Based in Montreal, and founded in 2005, they’ve set their sights on becoming a mid-sized gold producer. They're currently working on major projects in Canada, the U.S., and Mexico.

Their flagship project is the Cariboo Gold Project in British Columbia, covering around 155,000 hectares, and it’s the kind of place where there’s been enough gold historically dug up to make everyone involved hopeful that there’s still plenty left. Feasibility studies suggest it’s got serious potential.

Osisko’s also got a couple of other irons in the fire, including the Tintic Project in Utah and the San Antonio Gold Project down in Sonora, Mexico, where it’s hot, dusty, and, apparently, full of gold too. Alongside their mining ventures, they’ve also got a portfolio of marketable securities, and they’re still actively hunting for more precious and base metals.

Stock Market Performance

Despite Osisko Development Corp.'s best efforts with projects like Cariboo, Tintic, and San Antonio, the company has had a pretty rough ride in the stock market over the last few years. From December 2020 to October 2024, the stock managed to plummet from $18.00 to a rather sobering $2.26—a yearly drop of about 42%.

The Cariboo Gold Project

However, despite these setbacks, Osisko's latest results suggest there may still be hope on the horizon. The Cariboo Gold Project, for example, looks especially promising. With probable mineral reserves of 2.031 million ounces of gold and a rather handsome high-grade profile of 3.78 g/t Au, it just might be the golden ticket the company needs to turn things around, positioning it as a potential growth driver for the company.

Since the company based its projections on a gold price of $2,000 per ounce, the current spot price of $2,721 per ounce as of October 20, 2024, puts a fresh spin on things. At that $2,000 per ounce, they expected the Net Present Value (NPV) to hit C$821 million and the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) to reach 31.4%. But with gold trading much higher now, those numbers are set to improve well beyond the original forecasts.

Cariboo’s Financial Outlook

With production expected to peak at a healthy 223,000 ounces per year in Phase II and the all-in sustaining costs (AISC) comfortably low at $968/oz in Phase I and $886/oz in Phase II, Cariboo is shaping up to be a highly competitive player in this elevated price climate. The higher-than-expected gold price has only bolstered the project’s financial potential, turning it into an even more attractive investment. And there’s the extra bonus of growth potential with 1.57 million ounces of measured and indicated resources, plus another 1.71 million ounces of inferred resources waiting in the wings.

Tintic and Trixie Projects

Along with Cariboo’s strong outlook, the Tintic project, especially the Trixie mine, adds more fuel to Osisko’s growth potential. In 2024, the measured and indicated resources hit 245,000 tonnes at 19.11 g/t Au, a 57% bump in high-grade gold compared to 2023. This shows better resource definition and room for more expansion. High-grade drill results, like 610 g/t Au over 0.46 meters, also highlight the project's potential. Plus, Osisko operates in mining-friendly regions, giving them easy access to infrastructure and skilled labor, which should only strengthen their position.

Financial Strain and Dilution

While things are generally looking up for Osisko, there are a few clouds on the horizon that could dampen the mood. The Cariboo project, as promising as it is, comes with a rather eye-watering price tag—C$588 million in total, with C$137 million needed for Phase I and a chunky C$451 million for Phase II. That’s a lot of cash, and it could put a real squeeze on Osisko's finances, possibly forcing them to go cap in hand to raise more money, which means one thing: shareholder dilution. And it gets a bit worse. There are 27 million outstanding warrants and 7.1 million options floating around, which, if exercised, would dilute current shareholders by nearly 40%, taking the share count from a tidy 85.4 million to a much less tidy 119.5 million. And to top it off, their October 2024 private placement issued another 19.16 million shares, further diluting the pot.

Debt and Cash Reserves

Osisko’s debt load is another issue, with C$50 million in debt and just C$33.7 million in cash. While they’ve raised some capital, their cash reserves look small compared to the big expenses coming up, which could force them to take on more debt, adding financial pressure. Operational risks are also in play, especially at the Trixie project, where test mining has been paused multiple times, most recently in December 2023. Without a feasibility study, there are concerns about Trixie’s long-term potential, and small-scale test mining always runs the risk of cost overruns and inefficiencies.

San Antonio Project Uncertainty

There’s also some uncertainty hanging over Osisko’s San Antonio project in Mexico, which is currently under strategic review and doesn’t have a clear plan yet - this creates some doubt about how much it’ll contribute in the future. On top of that, Osisko’s economic forecasts are, naturally, pretty sensitive to changes in gold prices. That is, for San Antonio's, if gold drops below the assumed base case of $1,700/oz, the NPV and IRR for its projects would take a hit, making them less profitable. The reliance on inferred resources, which are more speculative than measured and indicated resources, adds even more risk. Plus, Osisko faces execution risk as it works to ramp up production to 350,000–500,000 ounces a year, which could bring operational challenges across different regions.

Valuation

Osisko appears to be undervalued compared to its peers, with a price-to-net asset value (P/NAV) multiple of just 0.4x, while others in the same space are trading between 0.7x and 1.1x. This suggests that, as Osisko inches closer to production, there’s some real potential for its stock to get a nice upward re-rating.

Gold Price Trends

When it comes to the notion of gold prices dropping below $1,700/oz anytime soon, I just don’t see it. In fact, I’d wager prices will continue to climb, and for a handful of very good reasons. First off, we’ve got geopolitical uncertainty practically oozing from every corner—particularly in places like Eastern Europe and the Middle East—which has a remarkable knack for making investors jittery. And when people get nervous, they tend to flock to gold, that trusty old "safe haven."

Another factor at play here is that central banks—particularly in countries like China, Russia, and Turkey—have been quietly but steadily increasing their gold buying. They're trying to reduce their reliance on the U.S. dollar and are using gold to hedge against currency risks. This surge in gold hoarding by central banks is, unsurprisingly, adding more fuel to the fire and nudging gold prices even higher.

Finally, one of the more curious reasons gold prices keep going up is, surprisingly, because not many investors seem interested in it—at least not yet. It turns out that, across North America and Europe, both private and professional investors have only sprinkled a tiny bit of gold into their portfolios, if they’ve added any at all. In fact, a survey found that most investment advisors had less than 1% of their portfolios allocated to gold.

Despite the steady rise in gold prices, these folks haven’t rushed to buy more, particularly when it comes to ETFs, those exchange-traded funds backed by gold. Eventually, when investors finally catch on to the upward trend, they’ll most likely pile in, driving prices even higher as they play catch-up.

Final Takeaway

I’d call Osisko Development Corp. a "Hold" for now. They’ve got some promising projects like Cariboo, especially with gold prices on the rise, but there are still some red flags—financial pressure, dilution, and operational risks that could hold back any quick gains. We’ll know more next month when they drop their earnings on November 13, 2024, and can get a better read on how their projects and recent fundraising are panning out.

data.title: XLP: Weak Growth Profile And Expensive Valuation
data.description:
Introduction

We wrote last an article on Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLP) back in April 2020. It was during the initial outbreak of COVID-19. At that time, we pointed out that XLP's portfolio of consumer staples stocks should be much more resilient in the widespread lockdown. Now that we are already in a post-pandemic world and the environment has changed considerably. It is time for us to analyze XLP again and provide our insights and recommendations.

ETF Overview

XLP has a portfolio of about 40 U.S. consumer staples stocks in the S&P 500 index. The fund has an inexpensive expense ratio of 0.09%. Funds such as iShares U.S. Consumer Staples ETF (IYK) charges a much higher expense ratio of 0.40%. XLP has better downside protection than the broader market as this sector is less impacted in an economic recession than many other sectors. However, it has a weak growth profile. Fortunately, thanks to receding inflation, profit margin should improve in 2025 and 2026. This should lead to better earnings growth in the near-term. The problem of owning XLP is its expensive valuation. Therefore, we think investors may want to wait on the sidelines.

Fund Analysis

XLP is resilient in market turmoil

The primary advantage of owning XLP is its resilience in economic recessions and market turmoil. In fact, the fund has a very low beta ratio of 0.58 relative to the S&P 500 index. For reader's information, beta ratio is a measure of the volatility of the fund. A lower beta ratio (less than 1) means it has lower volatility than the broader market. On the other hand, a beta ratio above 1 means that the fund has higher volatility than the broader market. XLP's low beta ratio means it has less downside risk than the broader market. In fact, XLP has declined much less during past economic recessions and market corrections. As can be seen from the chart below, the fund has only declined by about 33% during the Great Recession in 2008/2009. In contrast, the S&P 500 index has declined by about 55%. In the COVID-19 recession in 2020, XLP has declined by less than 20%. In contrast, the S&P 500 index suffered much higher casualty and declined by nearly 30%.

Consumer staples sector has lower revenue growth

XLP's resilience during market turmoil is unsurprising, as consumer staples sector offers essential services and products to consumers. Therefore, they are less impacted during economic recessions. However, XLP's stable business is a two-side sword. It can offer lower downside risk in an economic recession, but there are only so much essential items consumers need. As can be seen from the chart below, revenue growth rate for stocks in XLP's portfolio is expected to be in mid to low single-digit through 2026.

Fortunately, receding inflation will result in higher earnings growth

Despite XLP's lower revenue growth outlook, earnings growth rate should be above the revenue growth rate due to receding inflation. As we know, inflation has receded considerably and is expected to continue to trend downward. Therefore, we should see improving profit margins for stocks in XLP's portfolio. This should lead to higher earnings growth rate. As can be seen from the chart below, consensus earnings growth rate for stocks in XLP's portfolio is about 6.2% and 7.9% in 2025 and 2026, respectively. These mid- to high-single digit earnings growth rates are better than their low- to mid-single digit revenue growth rates.

Valuation remains expensive

Finally, we will take a look at XLP's valuation. As can be seen from the chart below, XLP's current forward P/E ratio is about 21.3x. This is towards the high end of its valuation range in the past 20 years. Therefore, we think XLP is quite expensive right now.

Long-term earnings growth rate decelerating

Besides its expensive valuation, XLP's long-term earnings growth (LTEG) rate is on a downward trend in the past 30 years. As can be seen from the chart below, its LTEG has declined from the peak of about 14% annually in 1998 to 7.6% recently. Therefore, we do not think XLP's current high valuation justifies its declining earnings growth rate.

Investor Takeaway

As we have discussed in our article, XLP has a weak growth profile and an expensive valuation. Therefore, we are unable to recommend this fund right now.

data.title: Climb Global Solutions: After Doubling In 6 Months, Future Growth Mostly Priced In
data.description:
I recommended Climb Global Solutions (NASDAQ:CLMB) to SA readers in May. Over the last five months, shares have nearly doubled on the back of strong operating results and general market momentum. Since shares have now surpassed my initial price target ($100), I am writing with an update to the thesis. Given higher potential downside risk implied by a higher multiple, and lowered upside, I think the risk-reward has become less attractive, and I'm downgrading Climb to HOLD before Q3 earnings.

Q2 results surpassed market expectations and confirmed my initial thesis. I had initially forecast a simple continuation of the prevailing growth rate (~15% CAGR). Q2 adjusted gross billings were up nearly 20% (excluding last year's acquisition of Data Solutions) and net sales grew 13%. As discussed on the Q2 earnings call, this growth was broadly based across the company's verticals and geographies. Security and data centers have been particularly strong, but as I noted in May, the company sells software from around 100 vendors, which enables them to drive consistent growth by focusing their sales efforts on verticals with strong sales momentum. They also evaluated 31 potential new vendors in Q2 and signed just three of them, for an acceptance rate below 10% - even lower than in 2023. These topline results modestly exceeded my initial expectations.

In Q2, topline growth drove a 100% increase in EPS and a 48% growth in adjusted EBITDA. This was based on much stronger operating leverage than I had initially forecast. Gross margins were basically flat, as expected. SG&A increased 12%, but almost all of that increase was due to the added operating costs at Data Solutions. Excluding the acquisition, SG&A grew from $11.6 million to $11.7, a ~1% increase. This suggests that I underestimated just how much Climb can grow EBITDA and earnings on the back of 15% revenue growth. Given that Climb recently completed its transition to a standard ERP system and expects some M&A synergies, I think we can expect this trend to continue (if less impressively) over the coming quarters.

The third leg of my thesis was M&A, which I expected to contribute an additional ~20% growth in 2024-2026. The company delivered on this promise in Q2 with the acquisition of Douglas Stewart Software & Services (DSS) for $20.3 million plus a potential close out of $4.2 million ($24.5 total). Climb paid just 4.6x DSS's TTM adjusted EBITDA of $5.3 million (by comparison, Climb had $6.9 million in adjusted EBITDA in Q2), which was about they had paid for Data Solutions, the basis for my initial M&A forecast. This should be immediately accretive, as explained during the Q&A session of the call:

Unidentified Analyst (longtime shareholder Howard Root)

Okay. And as I look at the number you gave is about a little over $5 million in adjusted EBITDA for the trailing 12 months, which looks to me like maybe a little bit around 20% of climbs. Is this about a 20% added to your bottom-line? Is it immediately accretive in that way? Or how do you look at it going forward in terms of percentage...

Dale Foster

It's immediately accretive to what we're doing. And they run a varying team 36 employees similar to our data solutions in Ireland -- extremely lean. They had a leading vendor of Citrix and this is a leading with Adobe. But right off the bat you'll see the results in Q3.

Unidentified Analyst

And I mean just on a ballpark number is it around it -- I'm not saying 20% increase to your bottom line but is it about a 20% increase to your adjusted EBITDA with this addition? Or am I missing something there?

Dale Foster

No you're right -- go ahead.

In other words, Climb's flywheel continues to spin. Organic revenue growth continues at around 15%. This is driving much faster EBITDA and EPS growth, as the CEO said in the earnings call, "reflecting the inherent operating leverage in our business model, which will further improve with the addition of DSS and global implementation of our ERP." On top of this, the business is generating enough cash (~$20 million / year) for more acquisitions. Add that up, and you comfortably get 35% annual topline growth. I think it is difficult to forecast exactly how much further operating leverage the business can generate. But given that management has repeatedly talked about doubling the size of the business and selling it without any discussion of further capital investment, I think it is quite possible that we get several more quarters like Q2, in which basically all of the additional revenue flows down to the bottom line. Q2 validates management's goal of doubling the business by 2026.

Looking at these exceptional operating results, the obvious question is: what is the remaining additional upside? After an incredible run over the last 2 years, can this stock go up further? It seems unreasonable to value this company using a DCF, since management has explicitly said that they think there is a cap on their future growth, and they are hoping to sell the company. Rather, I think we need to forecast 2026 earnings and then decide on a reasonable multiple. Here are my assumptions for 2025:

  • Organic (including DSS and DS acquisitions) revenue growth of 13%
  • Two more acquisitions comparable to DSS in size
  • Limited further operating leverage

Since DSS has comparable margins to Climb according to management, I estimate their revenue at around $70m, with a reported TTM growth rate of 10%. So, including the DSS acquisition and 13% organic growth, I estimate Climb's 2025 revenue at around $500 million. Given that Climb has $48m in cash, should generate $17m more by the end of 2025, and will probably spend $25m on DSS, they will have $40m left for acquisitions over the next year, which at comparable multiples, should generate an additional $100 million in 2026 revenue. This gives us forecast revenue of $500m in 2025 and $650 in 2026 and EPS of around $4 in 2025 and over $5 in 2026.

Climb's valuation has already risen to the industry averages. I don't yet view the valuation as worrisome, but given that the overall market is quite expensive, I think it is probably unreasonable to expect further multiple expansion from here. It is very hard to predict how the market will value Climb, but 35x earnings for a company growing at ~40% per year with no debt seems roughly fair to me.

This would give us a one-year price target of $140. That target, it should be said, is assuming that management's plan works. I continue to be impressed with their execution, but their assumptions are aggressive. It seems very unlikely to me that Climb radically exceeds them, which is what, I think, would be required for the stock to double again over the next 12 months.

Given general uncertainty about market valuations, the stock's incredible run, and the limited upside from here even if everything goes perfectly, I have trimmed my position. Since I still see further upside from here, I am holding some shares and giving the stock a HOLD rating.

When Q3 results are announced at the end of October, I will basically be looking to see if the three legs of the thesis continue to hold up. I doubt there will be another M&A announcement given how recently DSS was acquired. Instead, my focus will be on organic growth numbers. Revenue growth may slow somewhat; the business is somewhat seasonal and lumpy, and Q4 is usually the busiest quarter as companies spend their budgets and prepare for the next year. The revenue numbers are somewhat unpredictable and lumpy, as we saw after the big drop in shares after Q1. That provided a good buying opportunity, and I would be happy to pick up more shares if it happened again in Q3. Perhaps the most important thing I will be watching is margins. Management has touted their ERP rollout and promised some synergies from the recent acquisitions. Will these improvements materialize? If they do, higher margins could more than make up for slightly weaker revenue numbers. It seems like shares have paused their advance as the market waits for Q3 results. I would recommend that potential investors likewise wait for the print.

data.title: ARKK Should Not Be A Part Of Your Portfolio
data.description:
The ARK Innovation ETF (NYSEARCA:ARKK) is an almost $6 billion ETF. The ETF, with a 0.75% expense ratio, earns $45 million in annual fees for ARK Invest. That's despite the fact that the tech-focused ETF has only returned 15% over the past 5 years, while the S&P 500 has returned 95%, with most of those returns driven by tech.

As we'll see throughout this article, we expect the ETF's underperformance, despite strength in its core segment, to continue.

ARKK Overview

ARKK is a fund centered around chasing a goal of "disruptive innovation".

That's not necessarily a bad thing, and the company has an enormous host of technologies that it can invest in. We disagree with how transformation some of these investments are, such as "digital assets" and "smart contracts" which have been hyped up in popularity in the last few years, but show minimal growth potential or long-term user benefits.

Still, however, it's not an unreasonable goal.

ARKK Concentrated Holdings

ARKK has incredibly concentrated holdings in a few names that it believes are heavily undervalued.

The fund's top 5 holdings make up ~42% of the fund's holdings, showing how concentrated its performance is in these few names. Tesla is an investment that we've discussed recently as being several times fair value. We've discussed the details there, but ARKK's thesis centers around Tesla dominating markets such as self-driving, a market it's currently behind in.

Roku is the second-largest investment. Connected TVs are growing but Roku has continued to decline — not surprising in our view, given that traditional TV manufacturers have started to replace many of the features in their default options. We owned a Roku TV stick several years ago, but on recent TV purchases have had no need to buy or use one.

Then comes Coinbase to round out the top 3, one of the largest crypto exchanges. Crypto again dominated the 2021-2022 time period but has died down as most people have no use for it. Most crypto purchases are still for investing rather than spending, and we don't foresee any substantial growth for the currency.

With these top 3 investments, it's no surprise that ARKK is underperforming.

ARKK Fundamental Mistake

The fundamental mistake in our view is that ARKK is attempting to invest in innovation with a few large-cap limited companies.

The Innovator's Dilemma is a classic book that discusses that. Large companies are focused on their existing technologies and customers, while disruptive innovation is much more possible with smaller companies. That's because those smaller companies don't have existing business lines and customers that they're trying to protect.

The company is making the fundamental mistake of investing in large-cap companies that talk the talk because it doesn't have access to the best investments. For example, Waymo is well ahead of Tesla in self-driving, but ARKK doesn't have access to invest in Waymo. In our view, ARKK is reluctant to admit its mistakes from the past several years, and reorient its portfolio.

As a result, the company's largest investments are investments that we see as unlikely to outperform going forward. Given their concentrated weight, we expect that to hurt the company's future returns.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is that concentrated holdings can always break out unexpectedly. ARKK broke out in 2020 as a result. That could result in outperformance for the company over the short term. We see this as unlikely, but it's something that's worth paying close attention to in a changing market environment.

Conclusion

ARKK outperformed in 2020 as many individual investors chased the company and Cathie Wood rocketed to fame. Today, the fund still has a $6 billion value, driving tens of millions of dollars in annual fees for the fund. We still see the fund as a poor investment given its concentrated portfolio in poor investments and fundamental issues.

The fund is unable to invest in truly interesting opportunities such as Waymo that are being held by other large tech companies (Google, in this case). At the same time, a substantial percentage of the fund's existing assets are locked into poor investments that the fund is unwilling to sell. As a result, we expect future underperformance to continue, just as it has over the past 5 years.

data.title: Treasury Yields Snapshot: October 18, 2024
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The yield on the 10-year note ended October 18, 2024 at 4.08%, the 2-year note ended at 3.95%, and the 30-year at 4.38%.

Here is a table showing the yields' highs and lows and the FFR since 2007.

The next chart is an overlay of the daily performance of several Treasury bonds since the pre-recession days of equity market peaks, along with the Fed funds rate (FFR) since 2007.

A Long-Term Look at the 10-Year Treasury Yield

Here is a long look at the 10-year yield with a start date of 1965, well before the 1973 oil embargo that triggered the era of "stagflation" (economic stagnation with inflation).

Yield Curve

Here's the latest 10-2 spread. Typically, the spread goes negative for a period and then out of the red prior to recessions, and is thus considered a reliable leading indicator for recessions.

The lead time for recessions is quite a range - after going negative, recessions have begun anywhere from 16 to 62 weeks later. We also can see a false positive in 1998 where the spread went negative for a short period. For the 2009 recession, the spread went negative a couple of different times before rising.

If we use the first negative spread date as our starting point, the average number of weeks leading up to a recession is 37, or about nine months. If we use the last positive spread date after being negative before a recession, the average is 17 weeks, or 4.25 months and the median is 14 weeks, or 3.5 months.

For another perspective on the yield curve, the 10-3mo spread below utilizes an even shorter-term maturity.

The 30-Year Fixed Rate Mortgage

The latest Freddie Mac Weekly Primary Mortgage Market Survey put the 30-year fixed rate at 4.38%. Here is a long look back, courtesy of a FRED graph, of the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage average, which began in April of 1971.

Now let's see the 10-year against the S&P 500 with some notes on Federal Reserve intervention. Fed policy has been a major influence on market behavior.

For a long-term view of weekly Treasury yields, also focusing on the 10-year, see our latest Treasury Yields in Perspective update.

ETFs associated with Treasuries include: iShares 1-3 Year Treasury Bond ETF (SHY), iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF), and iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
